questions.stem: A patient in a long leg cast following tibial fracture reports severe, unrelenting pain rated 9/10 that is not relieved by opioids, along with paresthesia in the foot and tenseness of the cast. Which action is the NURSE'S PRIORITY?

options.text: Administer the PRN opioid analgesic and elevate the extremity above heart level
Assess the 6 P's and notify the provider immediately
Apply ice pack to reduce swelling and reassess in 30 minutes
Loosen the cast padding and document as a comfort measure

questions.rationale: This presentation suggests acute compartment syndrome: pain out of proportion to the injury, unrelieved by analgesia, and paresthesia (early sign of nerve ischemia). The 6 P's (Pain, Pressure, Paralysis, Paresthesia, Pallor, Pulselessness) guide assessment. The nurse must notify the provider IMMEDIATELY — compartment syndrome is a surgical emergency requiring fasciotomy within 6 hours to prevent permanent nerve and muscle damage. Elevating above heart level (A) decreases arterial perfusion, worsening ischemia. Ice (C) and cast loosening (D) delay definitive treatment.

distractorRationales.a: "Administer the PRN opioid analgesic and elevate the extremity above heart level" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Compartment syndrome 6 P's: pain out of proportion is the EARLIEST sign" points to medication toxicity. Medication safety depends on checking contraindications, labs, and adverse effects before giving or continuing the drug.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of medication-safety intervention. The stem cue "This presentation suggests acute compartment syndrome: pain out of proportion to the injury, unrelieved by analgesia" makes medication toxicity the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave medication toxicity untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Assess the 6 P's and notify the provider immediately" fits the safer response to the cue "The nurse must notify the provider IMMEDIATELY — compartment syndrome is a surgical emergency requiring fasciotomy within 6".

questions.takeaway: Compartment syndrome 6 P's: pain out of proportion is the EARLIEST sign. Fasciotomy must occur within 6 hours to prevent permanent damage.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A client with depression says: 'I don't see the point in trying anymore. Nothing I do matters.' Which nurse response is most therapeutic?

options.text: 'I understand. Sometimes things feel hopeless, but you have so much to live for.'
'Tell me more about what you mean when you say nothing matters.'
'Everyone feels that way sometimes. It will get better soon.'
'You shouldn't think like that. Let's focus on the positive things in your life.'

questions.rationale: Option B is an open-ended exploration that invites the client to elaborate, demonstrating active listening without judgment. It also screens for suicidal ideation — 'nothing I do matters' and 'no point in trying' may signal passive suicidal thinking. Option A uses false reassurance ('so much to live for') and assumes understanding without exploring further. Option C minimizes the client's experience ('everyone feels that way'). Option D is non-therapeutic confrontation that dismisses the client's feelings.

distractorRationales.a: The safer response addresses clinical safety risk; "'I understand. Sometimes things feel hopeless, but you have so much to live for.'" moves care toward routine care before bedside safety action.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave clinical safety risk untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "'Tell me more about what you mean when you say nothing matters.'" fits the safer response to the cue "therapeutic communication safety".
distractorRationales.d: "'You shouldn't think like that. Let's focus on the positive things in your life.'" misses the bedside risk. The cue "therapeutic communication safety" points to clinical safety risk. Nursing safety depends on matching the response to the active clinical risk before routine care.

questions.takeaway: Therapeutic communication: use open-ended statements to explore meaning, especially when statements may signal suicidal ideation.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with pneumonia who is receiving 4L/min via nasal cannula. The SpO2 is 91%. The nurse repositions the patient and administers the scheduled nebulizer treatment. Which finding would indicate the interventions were effective?

options.text: Respiratory rate decreases from 24 to 18 breaths/min and SpO2 increases to 95%
The patient reports less anxiety and requests to watch television
Adventitious breath sounds increase bilaterally after the nebulizer
The patient's skin color remains unchanged and RR remains at 24/min

questions.rationale: Effective respiratory interventions produce measurable objective improvements in gas exchange: SpO2 rising to ≥ 94-95% and respiratory rate normalizing toward < 20/min indicate improved ventilation and oxygenation. Decreased anxiety may reflect improved oxygenation but is subjective and non-specific. Increased breath sounds after a nebulizer can indicate mobilization of secretions (which is initially normal), not necessarily a therapeutic response. Unchanged SpO2 and RR indicate no meaningful improvement from the interventions.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "The patient reports less anxiety and requests to watch television" moves care toward routine observation before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.c: The cue "Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization" suggests oxygenation or ventilation, so "Adventitious breath sounds increase bilaterally after the nebulizer" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.d: The cue "work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring)" suggests oxygenation or ventilation, so "The patient's skin color remains unchanged and RR remains at 24/min" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.takeaway: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.
visualRationale.conclusion: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing vital signs in the pediatric unit. Which set of vital signs is within normal limits for a healthy 3-year-old toddler?
questions.rationale: Normal vital signs for a 3-year-old toddler: HR 80–130 bpm, RR 22–37 breaths/min, BP approximately 90–100/55–65 mmHg. HR 100, RR 26, BP 92/56 are all within expected ranges. Choice A has a bradycardic HR and adult-level BP. Choice C has tachycardia that could suggest distress, and RR 40 is elevated. Choice D has significant bradycardia. Key developmental milestone: vital signs normalize toward adult ranges with age — neonates have the highest HR and RR, lowest BP.

distractorRationales.a: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "HR 68, RR 14, BP 120/80" moves care toward routine care before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: "HR 55, RR 18, BP 110/72" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60" points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.takeaway: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.

visualRationale.title: Pediatric Nursing
visualRationale.caption: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.
visualRationale.conclusion: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.

questions.stem: A patient is scheduled for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy at 8:00 AM. Per current ASA NPO guidelines, what are the correct restrictions for solid food and clear liquids?

options.text: Nothing by mouth after midnight — no exceptions for any type of food or liquid
Solid food stopped by midnight; clear liquids (including milk) allowed until 4 hours before surgery
Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water, clear juice, black coffee) allowed until 2 hours before
No restrictions apply — laparoscopic procedures do not carry aspiration risk

questions.rationale: Current ASA NPO guidelines (2017, updated 2023): Solid foods, fried/fatty meals: NPO ≥8 hours. Light meals (toast, clear liquids): NPO ≥6 hours. Non-clear liquids including milk and formula: NPO ≥6 hours. Clear liquids (water, apple juice without pulp, clear carbonated beverages, black coffee or tea without cream, sports drinks): may be consumed up to 2 hours before elective procedures. Chewing gum and hard candy: treat as clear liquid — stop 2 hours before. These evidence-based guidelines replaced the outdated 'NPO after midnight' rule. The old rule increased patient discomfort, insulin resistance, and perioperative complications without reducing aspiration risk for clear fluids. High-risk patients (morbid obesity, GERD, pregnancy, gastroparesis, bowel obstruction) require individualized assessment and may need longer restriction.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization. The stem cue "The old rule increased patient discomfort, insulin resistance" makes unstable glucose physiology the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.b: The option can leave unstable glucose physiology untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water" fits the safer response to the cue "ASA NPO: solids ≥6-8 hrs before".
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses unstable glucose physiology; "No restrictions apply — laparoscopic procedures do not carry aspiration risk" moves care toward routine care before glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization.

questions.takeaway: ASA NPO: solids ≥6-8 hrs before; clear liquids until 2 hrs before. 'NPO after midnight' is outdated and harmful. Clear liquids: water, clear juice (no pulp), black coffee/tea.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is preparing to administer insulin glargine (Lantus) and insulin aspart (NovoLog) to a patient with type 1 diabetes before breakfast. Which action by the nurse requires IMMEDIATE correction?

options.text: Drawing up each insulin in a separate syringe
Mixing glargine and aspart in the same syringe before administration
Injecting the aspart subcutaneously 15 minutes before the meal
Rotating injection sites between the abdomen and thigh

questions.rationale: Insulin glargine (Lantus) is a long-acting basal insulin with a specific pH formulation. It should NEVER be mixed with any other insulin, including rapid-acting insulins. Mixing glargine with other insulins alters its pH, causing it to crystallize and unpredictably changes both insulins' absorption profiles. The result is an unreliable pharmacokinetic profile that can cause severe hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia. Each insulin must be drawn in a separate syringe and administered as separate injections. Drawing up separately is correct. Injecting aspart before meals (pre-prandial) is appropriate. Site rotation is correct practice.

distractorRationales.a: "Drawing up each insulin in a separate syringe" is accurate teaching, so it would not show the misunderstanding asked for in the stem. Drawing up each insulin in a separate syringe supports the teaching plan and medication safety.
distractorRationales.c: "Injecting the aspart subcutaneously 15 minutes before the meal" is accurate teaching, so it would not show the misunderstanding asked for in the stem. Injecting the aspart subcutaneously 15 minutes before the meal supports the teaching plan and medication safety.
distractorRationales.d: "Rotating injection sites between the abdomen and thigh" is accurate teaching, so it would not show the misunderstanding asked for in the stem. Rotating injection sites between the abdomen and thigh supports the teaching plan and medication safety.

questions.takeaway: Glargine (Lantus) = NEVER MIX with other insulins. It has its own pH and formulation. Mix-up can cause crystal formation and unpredictable glycemic effect. Use separate syringes, separate injections. Applies also to detemir (Levemir).
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for four patients on a medical-surgical unit. Which patient should the nurse assess first?

options.text: A 58-year-old with type 2 diabetes who reports feeling 'shaky' and has a blood glucose of 62 mg/dL
A 44-year-old post-appendectomy (day 2) requesting pain medication, pain rated 5/10
A 71-year-old with heart failure who gained 1 lb overnight and is on daily weights
A 33-year-old with a UTI who asks when the next dose of antibiotics is due

questions.rationale: The patient with a blood glucose of 62 mg/dL has symptomatic hypoglycemia ('shaky'), which is an acute, life-threatening condition requiring immediate intervention. Untreated hypoglycemia can rapidly progress to seizures, loss of consciousness, and brain injury. The other patients represent important but lower-acuity concerns: the post-op patient has manageable pain, the heart failure patient's weight gain needs assessment but is not immediately life-threatening, and the UTI patient's antibiotic timing is a scheduled task.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization. The stem cue "Apply the ABCs and Maslow's hierarchy: acute metabolic emergencies (hypoglycemia, airway compromise) always outrank scheduled pain requests, routine" makes unstable glucose physiology the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization. The stem cue "Untreated hypoglycemia can rapidly progress to seizures, loss of consciousness" makes unstable glucose physiology the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization. The stem cue "Apply the ABCs and Maslow's hierarchy: acute metabolic emergencies (hypoglycemia, airway compromise) always outrank scheduled pain requests, routine" makes unstable glucose physiology the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Apply the ABCs and Maslow's hierarchy: acute metabolic emergencies (hypoglycemia, airway compromise) always outrank scheduled pain requests, routine monitoring, and informational questions.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A public health nurse is investigating a cluster of foodborne illness cases in a community following a church potluck dinner. What is the correct sequence for outbreak investigation according to epidemiologic principles?

options.text: Implement control measures → identify cases → form hypothesis → test hypothesis
Identify and count cases → establish case definition → form hypothesis about source → implement control measures → test hypothesis
Notify media → quarantine all attendees → collect stool cultures → identify the organism
Form hypothesis first → identify cases → implement controls → notify CDC

questions.rationale: Epidemiologic outbreak investigation sequence (CDC framework): (1) Prepare for fieldwork; (2) Establish existence of an outbreak; (3) Verify diagnosis; (4) Construct a working case definition; (5) Find and count cases (line listing); (6) Perform descriptive epidemiology (person, place, time — epidemic curve); (7) Develop hypotheses; (8) Evaluate hypotheses (case-control or cohort study); (9) Implement control and prevention measures; (10) Communicate findings. Cases are identified and counted before hypotheses are formed; controls are implemented based on evidence, not assumption. Notification goes to appropriate health authorities, not media first.

distractorRationales.a: "Implement control measures → identify cases → form hypothesis → test hypothesis" misses the bedside risk. The cue "(3) Verify diagnosis" points to clinical safety risk. Maternal-fetal risk requires immediate safety measures because fetal oxygenation and maternal perfusion can deteriorate quickly.
distractorRationales.c: The cue "(3) Verify diagnosis" suggests clinical safety risk, so "Notify media → quarantine all attendees → collect stool cultures → identify the organism" would not correct the active problem. Maternal-fetal risk requires immediate safety measures because fetal oxygenation and maternal perfusion can deteriorate quickly.
distractorRationales.d: "Form hypothesis first → identify cases → implement controls → notify CDC" misses the bedside risk. The cue "(3) Verify diagnosis" points to clinical safety risk. Maternal-fetal risk requires immediate safety measures because fetal oxygenation and maternal perfusion can deteriorate quickly.

questions.takeaway: Outbreak investigation: identify cases first → case definition → descriptive epidemiology → hypothesis → test hypothesis → implement controls; evidence-based sequence prevents premature conclusions.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse has four patients to assess. Which patient should the nurse see FIRST, applying Maslow's hierarchy of needs?

options.text: A patient who is anxious about an upcoming procedure
A patient with a respiratory rate of 8 breaths/min and SpO2 of 88%
A patient who needs discharge teaching before going home
A patient asking for pain medication rated 4/10

questions.rationale: Maslow's hierarchy places physiological needs at the foundation. Oxygenation is the highest physiological priority. A patient with RR 8 and SpO2 88% is in respiratory distress — an immediate life-threatening concern that takes absolute priority. Anxiety (A) is a safety/psychological need. Discharge teaching (C) is a self-actualization/cognitive need. Pain rated 4/10 (D) is physiological but not immediately life-threatening compared to respiratory failure.

distractorRationales.a: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "A patient who is anxious about an upcoming procedure" moves care toward routine care before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. "A patient with a respiratory rate of 8 breaths/min and SpO2 of 88%" fits the safer response to the cue "Oxygenation is the highest physiological priority".
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "A patient asking for pain medication rated 4/10" moves care toward routine care before airway and breathing intervention.

questions.takeaway: Maslow hierarchy on NCLEX: airway/breathing/circulation (physiological) always come before safety, love/belonging, esteem, and self-actualization.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is completing an admission assessment on a 79-year-old patient transferred from a nursing facility. Which combination of factors identifies the HIGHEST fall risk?

options.text: Age >65 and female gender
History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic drop >20 mmHg on standing)
Mild bilateral hearing loss and BMI of 29
Mild arthritis in both knees and occasional evening confusion

questions.rationale: The highest fall risk constellation combines: (1) Prior fall history — the single strongest predictor of future falls in the elderly; (2) Polypharmacy with antihypertensives — alpha-blockers, diuretics, and calcium channel blockers commonly cause orthostatic hypotension and syncope; (3) Documented orthostatic hypotension — directly causes dizziness and falls upon position change. Both the Morse Fall Scale and STRATIFY risk tool weight prior falls most heavily. Key interventions: bed in lowest position, non-skid footwear, call light within reach, scheduled toileting (most falls occur during nighttime toileting), orthostatic vital signs before ambulation, and urgent medication review. Age and gender alone are poor predictors without functional factors.

distractorRationales.a: "Age >65 and female gender" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "The highest fall risk constellation combines: (1) Prior fall history — the single strongest predictor of future falls" raises concern for medication toxicity, so medication-safety intervention is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: "Mild bilateral hearing loss and BMI of 29" misses the bedside risk. The cue "The highest fall risk constellation combines: (1) Prior fall history — the single strongest predictor of future falls" points to medication toxicity. Medication safety depends on checking contraindications, labs, and adverse effects before giving or continuing the drug.
distractorRationales.d: "Mild arthritis in both knees and occasional evening confusion" normalizes a finding that needs reassessment. The cue "The highest fall risk constellation combines: (1) Prior fall history — the single strongest predictor of future falls" raises concern for medication toxicity, so medication-safety intervention is needed before reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension. Prior fall is the strongest single predictor. Target modifiable risk factors aggressively.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.
